title: The Science of Coffee: From Bean to Brew
summary: Discover how coffee beans transform into aromatic brews with our exploration into chemistry and physics. We delve into the natural processes, roasting techniques, and molecular changes that create your morning wake-up call.

sections.heading: Coffee Bean Formation
sections.body: The journey of coffee begins on the plant as a cherry, which turns into beans after ripening. These seeds undergo physical changes and chemical reactions before they are ready for roasting.

sections.heading: Roasting Processes
sections.body: During the dry and wet methods of coffee bean roasting, heat is applied to transform green beans into flavorful brown ones. Different temperatures lead to diverse chemical compounds that affect taste.
key_points: Roast levels from light to dark

sections.heading: Chemical Reactions in Coffee Making
sections.body: When water passes through roasted coffee grounds, it extracts flavors. This extraction involves dissolving and interacting with molecules like caffeine and chlorogenic acids.

sections.heading: Coffee Bean Sensory Experience
sections.body: Aside from flavor, roast level affects aroma and body texture. Smell contributes to the coffee's bouquet while grinding size influences extraction efficiency.

sections.heading: Environmental Impact of Coffee Production
sections.body: Cultivating coffee has environmental repercussions, including deforestation and water usage. Sustainable practices can mitigate these effects while ensuring the longevity of this cherished beverage.

key_facts: The caffeine content can range from about 60 to over 90 milligrams per cup.
Coffee beans have been found to help reduce the risk of Parkinson's disease.
